subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and talk about patterns they see when solving problems again and again.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child will look for and use patterns or shapes to help understand math.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use tools like blocks or drawings to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child will explain their thinking and listen to others' ideas about math problems.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and ideas in different ways to understand math better.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child will try to understand math problems and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Use simple shapes to form composite shapes.
description_plain: Your child can put simple shapes together to make new shapes.

subject: Math
description: Model shapes in the world by building shapes from components (e.g., use sticks and clay balls) and drawing shapes.
description_plain: Your child will build and draw shapes they see around them using parts like sticks and clay.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and compare two-dimensional and three-dimensional shapes, in different sizes and orientations, using informal language to describe their similarities, differences, parts (e.g., number of sides and vertices/corners), and other attributes (e.g., having sides of equal length).
description_plain: Your child can look at flat and solid shapes of different sizes and positions and talk about how they are alike and different, including parts like sides and corners.

subject: Math
description: Analyze, compare, create, and compose shapes.
description_plain: Your child can explore, compare, create, and put shapes together.

subject: Math
description: Identify shapes as two-dimensional (lying in a plane, flat) or three-dimensional (solid).
description_plain: Your child can tell if a shape is flat (2D) or solid (3D).

subject: Math
description: Correctly name shapes regardless of their orientation or overall size (e.g., circle, triangle, square, rectangle, rhombus, trapezoid, hexagon, cube, cone, cylinder, sphere).
description_plain: Your child can name shapes like circles, squares, and cubes no matter how they are turned or how big they are.

subject: Math
description: Describe objects in the environment using names of shapes, and describe the relative positions of these objects using terms such as above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.
description_plain: Your child can name shapes they see around them and describe where things are using words like above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe shapes.
description_plain: Identify and describe shapes.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects into given categories; count the number in each category and sort the categories by count. (Note: limit category counts to be less than or equal to 10.)
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups, count how many are in each group (up to 10), and put the groups in order from smallest to largest.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects and count the number of objects in each category.
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups and count how many are in each group.

subject: Math
description: Directly compare two objects with a measurable attribute in common to see which object has "more of" or "less of" the attribute, and describe the difference (e.g., directly compare the length of 10 cubes to a pencil and describe one as longer or shorter).
description_plain: Your child can compare two things by measuring and say which one has more or less of something, like length.

subject: Math
description: Describe measurable attributes of a single object (e.g., length and weight).
description_plain: Your child can describe things about an object that can be measured, like how long or heavy it is.

subject: Math
description: Describe and compare measurable attributes.
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare things that can be measured.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate understanding of addition and subtraction within 10 using place value.
description_plain: Your child understands adding and subtracting numbers up to 10 by using place value ideas.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child uses place value and number rules to add and subtract.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and additional ones by using objects, drawings and/or equations. Understand that these numbers are composed of ten ones and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones (e.g., 18 = 10 + 8).
description_plain: Your child can break apart numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some extra ones using objects, drawings, or equations.

subject: Math
description: Work with numbers 11 to 19 to gain foundations for place value.
description_plain: Your child works with numbers 11 to 19 to learn about place value.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 5.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 5.

subject: Math
description: For any number from 1 to 9, find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number (e.g., using fingers, objects, symbols, tally marks, drawings, or equation).
description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds with any number from 1 to 9 to make 10, using fingers or objects.

subject: Math
description: Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way (e.g., using fingers, objects, symbols, tally marks, drawings, expressions).
description_plain: Your child can break numbers up to 10 into pairs in more than one way using fingers, objects, or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems and add and subtract within 10.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction word problems and work with numbers up to 10.

subject: Math
description: Represent addition and subtraction concretely.
description_plain: Your child can show addition and subtraction using real objects.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
description_plain: Your child understands that addition means putting things together and subtraction means taking things apart or away.

subject: Math
description: Compare two numbers between 0 and 10 presented as written numerals.
description_plain: Your child can compare two numbers from 0 to 10 written as numerals and say which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group. (Include groups with up to ten objects.)
description_plain: Your child can tell if one group of up to 10 objects has more, less, or the same number as another group by matching or counting.

subject: Math
description: Compare numbers.
description_plain: Compare numbers.

subject: Math
description: Count to answer questions about "How many?" when 20 or fewer objects are arranged in a line, a rectangular array, or a circle, or as many as 10 things in a scattered configuration; given a number from 1 to 20, count out that many objects.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 20 objects arranged in different ways and can count out a specific number of objects from 1 to 20.

subject: Math
description: Understand that each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger (hierarchical inclusion).
description_plain: Your child understands that each number you say when counting is one more than the number before.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the last number name said tells the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of their arrangement or the order in which they were counted (cardinality).
description_plain: Your child knows that the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.

subject: Math
description: When counting objects, say the number names in the standard order, pairing each object with one and only one number name and each number name with one and only one object (one to one correspondence).
description_plain: Your child can count objects by saying number names in order, matching one number to each object.

subject: Math
description: Understand the relationship between numbers and quantities; connect counting to cardinality.
description_plain: Your child understands how numbers and counting relate to the amount of things.

subject: Math
description: Count to tell the number of objects.
description_plain: Your child can count objects to find out how many there are.

subject: Math
description: Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0 to 20 (with 0 representing a count of no objects).
description_plain: Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and match numbers to groups of objects.

subject: Math
description: Count forward from a given number other than one, within the known sequence (e.g., "Starting at the number 5, count up to 11.").
description_plain: Your child can start counting from any number other than one and continue in order.

subject: Math
description: Count to 100 by ones and by tens.
description_plain: Your child can count to 100 by ones and by tens.

subject: Math
description: Know number names and the count sequence.
description_plain: Your child knows number names and the order of counting.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated ideas when solving math problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can find and use patterns or structures to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use tools like rulers or counters to help with math tasks.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking about math problems and listen to others’ ideas.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and math ideas in different ways, both with objects and in their head.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand math problems and keep trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, fourths, and quarters. Describe the whole as two of, or four of the shares. Understand that decomposing into more equal shares creates smaller shares.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and use words like halves and quarters to describe them.

subject: Math
description: Compose two-dimensional shapes or three-dimensional shapes to create a composite shape.
description_plain: Your child can put flat or solid shapes together to make new shapes.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between defining attributes (triangles are closed and 3 sided) versus non-defining attributes (color, orientation, overall size) for two-dimensional shapes; build and draw shapes that possess defining attributes.
description_plain: Your child can tell what makes a shape a triangle or other shape by its sides and corners, and can draw or build these shapes.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child can think about shapes and their features.

subject: Math
description: Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to three categories; ask and answer questions about the total number of data points, how many in each category, and how many more or less are in one category than in another.
description_plain: Your child can collect and organize data into up to three groups and ask and answer questions about how many are in each group and how the groups compare.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Identify coins by name and value (pennies, nickels, dimes and quarters).
description_plain: Your child can recognize pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters and know their values.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the hour and half-hour using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Work with time and money.
description_plain: Work with time and money.

subject: Math
description: Express the length of an object as a whole number of length units, by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end; understand that the length measurement of an object is the number of same-size length units that span it with no gaps or overlaps. (Limit to contexts where the object being measured is spanned by a whole number of length units with no gaps or overlaps.)
description_plain: Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller, same-sized objects end to end without gaps or overlaps.

subject: Math
description: Order three objects by length. Compare the lengths of two objects indirectly by using a third object.
description_plain: Your child can put three objects in order from shortest to longest and compare two objects’ lengths by using a third object.

subject: Math
description: Measure lengths indirectly and by iterating length units.
description_plain: Your child can measure lengths by using smaller units repeatedly or by measuring indirectly.

subject: Math
description: Subtract multiples of 10 in the range of 10 to 90 (positive or zero differences), using objects or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction. Relate the strategy to a written form.
description_plain: Your child can subtract numbers like 10, 20, up to 90 using objects or drawings and explain how they did it.

subject: Math
description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count.
description_plain: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than any two-digit number without counting.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate understanding of addition within 100, connecting objects or drawings to strategies based on place value (including multiples of 10), properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction. Relate the strategy to a written form.
description_plain: Your child can add numbers up to 100 by using objects or drawings and understand place value and how addition and subtraction relate.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of tens and ones to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, and <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at the tens and ones, and use symbols like >, =, and < to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine tens (and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 10, 20, 30, etc., mean groups of tens and zero ones.

subject: Math
description: The numbers from 11 to 19 are composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers from 11 to 19 are made of one ten and some ones.

subject: Math
description: 10 can be thought of as a group of ten ones — called a "ten".
description_plain: Your child understands that 10 is a group of ten ones called a 'ten.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent groups of tens and ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that two-digit numbers show how many tens and ones they have, including special cases like teens and multiples of ten.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Understand place value.

subject: Math
description: Count to 120 by 1's, 2's, and 10's starting at any number less than 100. In this range, read and write numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.
description_plain: Your child can count to 120 by ones, twos, and tens starting from any number less than 100, and can read, write, and show numbers with objects.

subject: Math
description: Extend the counting sequence.
description_plain: Extend the counting sequence.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation relating three whole numbers (e.g., determine the unknown number that makes the equation true in each of the equations 8 + ? = 11, 5 = ? - 3, 6 + 6 = ?).
description_plain: Your child can figure out the missing number in an addition or subtraction problem with three numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the meaning of the equal sign, and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false (e.g., Which of the following equations are true and which are false? 6 + 1 = 6 - 1, 7 = 8 - 1, 5 + 2 = 2 + 5, 4 + 1 = 5 + 2).
description_plain: Your child understands what the equal sign means and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.

subject: Math
description: Work with addition and subtraction equations.
description_plain: Your child can work with addition and subtraction equations.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 10.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 10.

subject: Math
description: Relate counting to addition and subtraction (e.g., by using counting on 2 to add 2).
description_plain: Your child can use counting to help with addition and subtraction, like counting on to add.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 10.
description_plain: Add and subtract within 10.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction as an unknown-addend problem within 20 (e.g., subtract 10 - 8 by finding the number that makes 10 when added to 8).
description_plain: Your child can solve subtraction problems by thinking about what number added to the smaller number makes the bigger number.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations (commutative and associative properties of addition) as strategies to add and subtract through 20. (Students need not use formal terms for these properties.)
description_plain: Your child can use patterns in addition, like changing the order of numbers, to help add and subtract numbers up to 20.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child understands and uses the rules for adding and subtracting, and knows how they are connected.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20 (e.g., by using objects, drawings, and/or equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add three numbers with a total of 20 or less using objects, drawings, or equations with a missing number.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 20 to solve word problems with unknowns in all positions (e.g., by using objects, drawings, and/or equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using addition and subtraction within 20 by using objects, drawings, or simple equations with a missing number.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can show and solve problems using addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child notices and explains patterns when solving math problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child looks for and uses the way things are organized or structured in math.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses the best tools to solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child explains their math thinking and listens to others to understand their ideas.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child thinks about math problems both with numbers and in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child works through math problems carefully and doesn’t give up easily.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, thirds, fourths, half of, third of, fourth of, and describe the whole as two halves, three thirds, or four fourths. Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into equal parts like halves, thirds, or fourths and describe these parts and the whole.

subject: Math
description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size rectangles and count to find the total number of rectangles.
description_plain: Your child can divide a rectangle into equal rows and columns and count how many smaller rectangles there are.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe specified attributes of two-dimensional and three-dimensional shapes, according to the number and shape of faces, number of angles, and the number of sides and/or vertices. Draw two-dimensional shapes based on the specified attributes (e.g., triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, and hexagons).
description_plain: Your child can identify and describe shapes by their sides, angles, faces, and corners, and can draw shapes like triangles and hexagons.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child understands shapes and their features and can think about how they are alike and different.

subject: Math
description: Draw a picture graph and a bar graph (with single-unit scale) to represent a data set with up to four categories. Solve simple put-together, take-apart, and compare problems using information presented in the graph.
description_plain: Your child can make picture and bar graphs with up to four categories and use them to solve simple problems.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths of several objects to the nearest whole unit, or by making repeated measurements of the same object. Show the measurements by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in whole-number units.
description_plain: Your child can measure several objects, record the lengths on a line plot, and organize the data.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving collections of money, including dollar bills, quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies. Record the total using $ and ¢ appropriately.
description_plain: Your child can solve money problems using dollars and coins and write the total amount correctly.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time from analog and digital clocks to the nearest five minutes, using a.m. and p.m.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest five minutes on analog and digital clocks, using a.m. and p.m.

subject: Math
description: Work with time and money.
description_plain: Work with time and money.

subject: Math
description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points corresponding to the numbers 0, 1, 2, …, and represent whole-number sums and differences within 100 on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can show numbers on a number line and use it to add and subtract numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve word problems involving lengths that are given in the same unit.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about lengths using addition and subtraction within 100, with drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Relate addition and subtraction to length.
description_plain: Your child understands how addition and subtraction relate to measuring length.

subject: Math
description: Measure to determine how much longer one object is than another, expressing the length difference in terms of a standard length unit.
description_plain: Your child can measure how much longer one object is than another using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Estimate lengths using units of inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.
description_plain: Your child can estimate lengths using inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object twice, using different standard length units for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen. Understand that depending on the size of the unit, the number of units for the same length varies.
description_plain: Your child can measure the same object with two different units and explain how the size of the unit changes the number of units needed.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object by selecting and using appropriate tools (e.g., ruler, meter stick, yardstick, measuring tape).
description_plain: Your child can choose and use tools like rulers or measuring tapes to measure length.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate lengths using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Explain why addition and subtraction strategies work, using place value and the properties of operations. (Explanations may be supported by drawings or objects.)
description_plain: Your child can explain why their ways of adding and subtracting work by using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number in the range of 100 and 900, and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number in the range of 100 and 900.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add or subtract 10 or 100 in their head with numbers between 100 and 900.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate understanding of addition and subtraction within 1000, connecting objects or drawings to strategies based on place value (including multiples of 10), properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction. Relate the strategy to a written form.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using objects or drawings and explain their steps in writing.

subject: Math
description: Add up to three two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can add up to three two-digit numbers using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 100 using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 100 using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of place value to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on meanings of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at the hundreds, tens, and ones, and use the symbols >, =, and < to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers up to 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 1000 in regular form, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count within 1000; skip count by 5's, 10's and 100's.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 1000 and skip count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds (and 0 tens and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, 300, etc., mean one, two, three, and more hundreds with zero tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: 100 can be thought of as a group of ten tens—called a "hundred."
description_plain: Your child understands that 100 is made of ten groups of ten, called a 'hundred.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent groups of hundreds, tens, and ones (e.g., 706 equals 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones and also equals 70 tens and 6 ones). Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that a three-digit number shows how many hundreds, tens, and ones it has, like 706 means 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Understand place value.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays (with up to 5 rows and 5 columns). Write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rows and columns and write an equation showing equal groups added together.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members (e.g., by pairing objects or counting them by 2's).
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number by pairing them or counting by twos.

subject: Math
description: Work with equal groups of objects to gain foundations for multiplication.
description_plain: Your child works with equal groups of objects to start learning about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 20. By the end of Grade 2, know from memory all sums of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 20 and knows all sums of two one-digit numbers by memory.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20.
description_plain: Add and subtract within 20.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one-step word problems. Use addition to solve two-step word problems using single-digit addends. Represent a word problem as an equation with a symbol for the unknown.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple word problems using addition and subtraction within 100 and write equations with a symbol for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can use addition and subtraction to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child notices patterns when solving problems and explains them.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child looks for ways things are organized or connected in math problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses tools like rulers or calculators to help solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child explains their math thinking and listens to others’ ideas to decide if they make sense.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child thinks about math problems both with numbers and in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child understands math problems and keeps trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition shapes into b parts with equal areas. Express the area of each part as a unit fraction 1/b of the whole. (Grade 3 expectations are limited to fractions with denominators b = 2, 3, 4, 6, 8.)
description_plain: Your child can divide shapes into equal parts and say the size of each part as a fraction like 1/2 or 1/4.

subject: Math
description: Understand that shapes in different categories (e.g., rhombuses, rectangles, and others)may share attributes (e.g., having four sides), and that the shared attributes can define a larger category (e.g., quadrilaterals). Recognize rhombuses, rectangles, and squares as examples of quadrilaterals, and draw examples quadrilaterals that do not belong to any of these subcategories.
description_plain: Your child knows that shapes like rhombuses, rectangles, and squares share some features and belong to bigger groups like quadrilaterals.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child can think about shapes and their features.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving perimeters of plane figures and areas of rectangles, including finding the perimeter given the side lengths, finding an unknown side length. Represent rectangles with the same perimeter and different areas or with the same area and different perimeters.
description_plain: Your child can find the perimeter and area of rectangles and solve problems about missing side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Understand that rectilinear figures can be decomposed into non-overlapping rectangles and that the sum of the areas of these rectangles is identical to the area of the original rectilinear figure. Apply this technique to solve problems in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can break complex shapes into rectangles and add their areas to find the total area.

subject: Math
description: Use tiling to show that the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths a and b + c is the sum of a × b and a × c. Use area models to represent the distributive property in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child uses area models to show how multiplying side lengths relates to adding parts of a shape.

subject: Math
description: Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles with whole-number side lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems, and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child multiplies side lengths to find the area of rectangles and uses this to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling it, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.
description_plain: Your child finds the area of a rectangle by counting squares or multiplying side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Relate area to the operations of multiplication and addition.
description_plain: Your child understands that area relates to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Measure areas by counting unit squares (e.g., square cm, square m, square in, square ft, and improvised units).
description_plain: Your child measures area by counting square units like square centimeters or square inches.

subject: Math
description: A plane figure which can be covered without gaps or overlaps by n unit squares is said to have an area of n square units.
description_plain: Your child knows that if a shape can be covered exactly by n squares, its area is n square units.

subject: Math
description: A square with side length 1 unit, called "a unit square," is said to have "one square unit" of area, and can be used to measure area.
description_plain: Your child understands that a square with sides one unit long is called a unit square and measures one square unit of area.

subject: Math
description: Understand area as an attribute of plane figures and understand concepts of area measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands what area means and how to measure it for flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: Understand concepts of area and perimeter.
description_plain: Your child learns about measuring shapes, including their area and perimeter.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths using rulers marked with halves and fourths of an inch to the nearest quarter-inch. Show the data by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in appropriate units— whole numbers, halves, or quarters.
description_plain: Your child measures lengths using rulers marked in halves and quarters of an inch and shows the results on a line plot.

subject: Math
description: Create a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child makes picture and bar graphs to show data and solves problems about how many more or less between categories.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses of objects using metric units. (Excludes compound units such as cm3 and finding the geometric volume of a container.) Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve one-step word problems involving masses or volumes that are given in the same units. Excludes multiplicative comparison problems (problems involving notions of "times as much").
description_plain: Your child measures and estimates liquid amounts and weights using metric units and solves simple math problems with these measurements.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving money through $20.00, using symbols $, ".", ₵.
description_plain: Your child solves money word problems with amounts up to $20 using dollar and cent symbols.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute and measure time intervals in minutes. Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals in minutes (e.g., representing the problem on a number line diagram).
description_plain: Your child tells and writes time to the nearest minute and solves problems about adding or subtracting time intervals.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement.
description_plain: Your child solves problems that involve measuring things like time, money, length, and weight.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about their size. Understand that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify conclusions.
description_plain: Your child compares two fractions with the same top or bottom number by thinking about their size and uses >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Express whole numbers as fractions, and recognize fractions that are equivalent to whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write whole numbers as fractions and recognize when fractions are equal to whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and generate simple equivalent fractions. Explain why the fractions are equivalent.
description_plain: Your child can find simple fractions that are equal and explain why they are the same.

subject: Math
description: Understand two fractions as equivalent if they have the same relative size compared to 1 whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that two fractions are equal if they represent the same part of a whole.

subject: Math
description: Explain equivalence of fractions in special cases, and compare fractions by reasoning about their size.
description_plain: Your child can explain when two fractions are equal and compare fractions by thinking about their size.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction 1/b as a special type of fraction that can be referred to as a unit fraction (e.g. ½, ¼).
description_plain: Your child understands that fractions like 1/2 or 1/4 are called unit fractions because they show one equal part of a whole.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction a/b on a number line diagram by marking off a lengths 1/b from 0. Understand that the resulting interval has size a/b and that its endpoint locates the number a/b on the number line including values greater than 1.
description_plain: Your child can show a fraction like a/b on a number line by marking off equal parts and locating the fraction’s position, even if it’s more than 1.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction 1/b on a number line diagram by defining the interval from 0 to 1 as the whole and partitioning it into b equal parts. Understand that each part has size 1/b and that the end point of the part based at 0 locates the number 1/b on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show the fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into equal parts and identifying one part.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction as a number on the number line; represent fractions on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child understands fractions as numbers and can place them correctly on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction (1/b) as the quantity formed by one part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts; understand a fraction a/b as the quantity formed by a parts of size 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like 1/b means one part of a whole divided into b equal parts, and a/b means a parts of size 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Understand fractions as numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands that fractions are numbers that show parts of a whole.

subject: Math
description: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10 in the range 10 to 90 using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations (e.g., 9 × 80, 5 × 60).
description_plain: Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by multiples of 10 (like 9 × 80) using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 1000 using strategies and algorithms based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using strategies based on place value and math facts.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100 using place value.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child uses their understanding of place value and math rules to solve problems with bigger numbers.

subject: Math
description: When solving problems, assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can check if answers to problems make sense by estimating and using mental math like rounding.

subject: Math
description: Identify patterns in the addition table and the multiplication table and explain them using properties of operations (e.g. observe that 4 times a number is always even, and explain why 4 times a number can be decomposed into two equal addends).
description_plain: Your child can find and explain patterns in addition and multiplication tables using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Solve two-step word problems using the four operations. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Utilize understanding of the Order of Operations when there are no parentheses.
description_plain: Your child can solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division and check their answers by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving the four operations, and identify and explain patterns in arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child can solve math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, and can find and explain patterns in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply and divide within 100. By the end of Grade 3, know from memory all multiplication products through 10 × 10 and division quotients when both the quotient and divisor are less than or equal to 10.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide numbers up to 100 quickly and knows all multiplication and division facts up to 10 × 10 by memory.

subject: Math
description: Multiply and divide within 100.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem (e.g., find 32 ÷ 8 by finding the number that makes 32 when multiplied by 8).
description_plain: Your child understands division as finding a missing number that, when multiplied, gives the original number.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide. Properties include commutative and associative properties of multiplication and the distributive property. (Students do not need to use the formal terms for these properties.)
description_plain: Your child uses math rules to help multiply and divide, like changing the order or grouping of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand properties of multiplication and the relationship between multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child understands how multiplication works and how it is connected to division.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation relating three whole numbers For example, determine the unknown number that makes the equation true in each of the equations 8 × ? = 48, 5 = ? ÷ 3, 6 × 6 = ?.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division problems without needing special math words.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about equal groups, arrays, or measurements using multiplication and division, and write equations with a letter for the unknown.

subject: Math
description: Interpret whole number quotients of whole numbers (e.g., interpret 56 ÷ 8 as the number of objects in each group when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 groups, or as a number of groups when 56 objects are partitioned into equal groups of 8 objects each).
description_plain: Your child can explain division as splitting objects into equal groups or finding how many groups there are.

subject: Math
description: Interpret products of whole numbers as the total number of objects in equal groups (e.g., interpret 5 × 7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each).
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as finding the total number of objects in equal groups.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving whole number multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using multiplication and division with whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child looks for and explains repeated patterns or steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child notices and uses the way things are organized or structured to solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses tools like rulers or calculators to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child explains their thinking and listens to others to understand and improve math ideas.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child thinks about numbers and problems in different ways to understand and solve them.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child works to understand problems and keeps trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a line of symmetry for a two-dimensional figure as a line across the figure such that the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts. Identify line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry on flat shapes by folding them to see if both sides match.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size (e.g., understand right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles).
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes by looking for parallel or perpendicular lines and by naming triangles as right, acute, or obtuse based on their angles.

subject: Math
description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can draw points, lines, line segments, rays, and different types of angles, and find these parts in flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shapes by properties of their lines and angles.
description_plain: Your child can draw and identify lines and angles and group shapes based on their lines and angles.

subject: Math
description: Understand angle measures as additive. (When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts.) Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram within mathematical problems as well as problems in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands that the size of a big angle is the sum of smaller angles inside it and can solve problems to find missing angles.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Sketch angles of specified measure.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles using a protractor and draw angles with a given size.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through n one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.
description_plain: Your child knows that an angle made by turning through n one-degree steps measures n degrees.

subject: Math
description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where the two rays intersect the circle. An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a "one-degree angle," and can be used to measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands that an angle is measured by how much it turns around a point, with one degree being 1/360 of a full turn.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement:
description_plain: Your child knows that angles are shapes made by two rays sharing a point and learns how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: Understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
description_plain: Your child learns about angles and how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements using fractions like 1/2 or 1/4 and solve addition or subtraction problems using the plot.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in mathematical problems and problems in real-world contexts including problems with unknown side lengths.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the area and perimeter of rectangles and solve problems, including those with missing side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations to solve word problems and problems in real-world context involving distances, intervals of time (hr, min, sec), liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including decimals and problems involving fractions with like denominators, and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using a variety of representations, including number lines that feature a measurement scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with measurements like distance, time, volume, weight, and money, including decimals and fractions, and can show these measurements on number lines.

subject: Math
description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit and in a smaller unit in terms of a larger unit.
description_plain: Your child understands how different units of measurement relate to each other (like kilometers to meters or hours to minutes) and can change measurements from bigger units to smaller ones and record these changes in a table.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems that involve measuring things and changing measurements from larger units to smaller units.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Understand that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimal numbers up to hundredths place by thinking about their size and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal, but only when the decimals refer to the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 (tenths) or 100 (hundredths), and locate these decimals on a number line.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions with denominators 10 or 100 as decimals and find where these decimals belong on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 (tenths) and 100 (hundredths).
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction with denominator 10 into an equal fraction with denominator 100 and use this to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100.

subject: Math
description: Understand decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
description_plain: Your child can understand how fractions and decimals are related and can compare decimal numbers.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems where a whole number is multiplied by a fraction.

subject: Math
description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of a unit fraction 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a whole number by a fraction. In general, n × a/b = n × a/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction a/b by a whole number n means multiplying the numerator a by n, and can use this to multiply whole numbers by fractions.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of a unit fraction 1/b. In general, a/b = a × 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction a/b means adding the unit fraction 1/b together a times.

subject: Math
description: Build fractions from unit fractions.
description_plain: Your child can build fractions by adding together unit fractions like 1/4 or 1/8.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add or subtract fractions with the same denominator using drawings or fraction models.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators (e.g., by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction and/or by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction).
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract mixed numbers with the same denominator by using properties of operations or by changing mixed numbers into improper fractions.

subject: Math
description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way (e.g., 3/8 = 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8; 3/8 = 2/8 + 1/8; 2 1/8 = 1 + 1 + 1/8 + or 2 1/8 = 8/8 + 8/8 + 1/8).
description_plain: Your child can break a fraction into smaller fractions with the same denominator in different ways and explain these using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that adding and subtracting fractions means putting parts together or taking parts apart from the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of unit fractions (1/b).
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction with numerator greater than 1 can be seen as a sum of unit fractions.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understanding of multiplication to multiply a whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a whole number by a fraction using what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Record the results of comparisons with symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions.
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions using >, =, or < and explain why one fraction is bigger, smaller, or equal to the other.

subject: Math
description: Understand that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same size whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that you can only compare fractions if they refer to the same size whole.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators (e.g., by creating common denominators or numerators and by comparing to a benchmark fraction).
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions with different numerators and denominators by finding common denominators or numerators or by comparing to a known fraction.

subject: Math
description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n × a)/(n × b) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to understand and generate equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child can explain why two fractions are equal by using pictures to show how the parts are different sizes but the fractions are the same, and can find and create equivalent fractions, including those greater than 1.

subject: Math
description: Extend understanding of fraction equivalence and ordering.
description_plain: Your child understands how to tell if two fractions are equal and can put fractions in order from smallest to largest.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate understanding of division by finding whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by one-digit numbers and find the whole number answer and remainder.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a number with up to four digits by a one-digit number and multiply two two-digit numbers using place value and properties of multiplication, and explain their work with equations or models.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using a standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract large whole numbers using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of place value to solve problems with large numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round multi-digit whole numbers to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round large numbers to any place value, like the nearest ten, hundred, or thousand.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form. Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can read and write large whole numbers in different ways and compare two numbers by looking at the value of each digit, using >, =, or < to show which is bigger.

subject: Math
description: Apply concepts of place value, multiplication, and division to understand that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a multi-digit number, each digit is ten times the value of the digit to its right.

subject: Math
description: Generalize place value understanding for multi-digit whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works for numbers with more than one digit.

subject: Math
description: When solving problems, assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can check if answers to problems make sense by using mental math and estimation like rounding.

subject: Math
description: Generate a number pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself and explain the pattern informally (e.g., given the rule "add 3" and the starting number 1, generate terms in the resulting sequence and observe that the terms appear to alternate between odd and even numbers).
description_plain: Your child can create number patterns following a rule, notice patterns that aren’t obvious at first, and explain them in simple terms.

subject: Math
description: Generate and analyze patterns.
description_plain: Your child can create and study patterns in numbers or shapes.

subject: Math
description: Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1 to 100 and understand that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors.
description_plain: Your child can find all pairs of numbers that multiply to a number between 1 and 100 and understand that the number is a multiple of each of those factors.

subject: Math
description: Gain familiarity with factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child is getting to know how factors and multiples work.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step word problems using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Understand how the remainder is a fraction of the divisor. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, including problems where the remainder is part of the answer, and write equations with letters for unknowns.

subject: Math
description: Multiply or divide within 1000 to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison (e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, distinguishing multiplicative comparison from additive comparison).
description_plain: Your child can multiply or divide numbers up to 1000 to solve word problems that compare amounts by multiplication, using drawings and equations with unknowns.

subject: Math
description: Represent verbal statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations. Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison (e.g., 35 is the number of objects in 5 groups, each containing 7 objects, and is also the number of objects in 7 groups, each containing 5 objects).
description_plain: Your child can turn word problems about comparing groups into multiplication equations and understand that multiplication can show how groups compare.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child looks for and explains patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child notices how math problems are organized and uses that to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses the best tools, like rulers or calculators, to solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking clearly and understand other people’s ideas about math.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems both with numbers and in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child tries to understand math problems and keeps working on them until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.
description_plain: Your child can group flat shapes based on their properties in a way that shows which shapes belong to bigger or smaller groups.

subject: Math
description: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.
description_plain: Your child understands that if a shape has certain features, then all shapes in smaller groups within that category also have those features.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures into categories based on their properties.
description_plain: Your child can group flat shapes into categories based on their features.

subject: Math
description: Represent real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on a graph in the first quadrant and understand what the points mean in real-life or math problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand and describe a coordinate system as perpendicular number lines, called axes, that intersect at the origin (0 , 0). Identify a given point in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane using an ordered pair of numbers, called coordinates. Understand that the first number (x) indicates the distance traveled on the horizontal axis, and the second number (y) indicates the distance traveled on the vertical axis.
description_plain: Your child can understand a coordinate plane as two number lines crossing at zero and can find points in the first section by using pairs of numbers that show how far to go right and up.

subject: Math
description: Graph points on the coordinate plane to solve mathematical problems as well as problems in real-world context.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on a coordinate plane to help solve math problems and real-life questions.

subject: Math
description: Understand volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two non-overlapping right rectangular prisms, applying this technique to solve mathematical problems and problems in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can find the total volume of shapes made by joining two rectangular boxes without overlapping, and use this to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand and use the formulas V = l × w × h and V = B × h, where in this case B is the area of the base (B = l × w), for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole-number edge lengths to solve mathematical problems and problems in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the volume of rectangular boxes by multiplying length, width, and height, and apply this to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. Represent threefold whole-number products as volumes (e.g., to represent the associative property of multiplication).
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of a rectangular box by filling it with unit cubes and show that multiplying the edges or the base area by height gives the same volume.

subject: Math
description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve mathematical problems and problems in real-world contexts involving volume.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume relates to multiplication and addition and can solve problems about volume in math and real life.

subject: Math
description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and improvised units.
description_plain: Your child can measure volume by counting unit cubes and using units like cubic centimeters, inches, feet, or other units.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child understands that a solid filled exactly with a certain number of unit cubes has a volume equal to that number of cubes.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child knows that a cube with sides of one unit has a volume of one cubic unit and can use it to measure volume.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume is a property of solid shapes and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: Understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
description_plain: Your child learns what volume means and how it connects to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/8, ½, ¾). Use operations on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements using fractions like 1/8 or 1/2 and use fraction math to solve problems from the plot.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given measurement system, and use these conversions in solving multi-step, real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child can change measurements from one unit to another within the same system (like cm to m) and use these changes to solve multi-step real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Convert like measurement units within a given measurement system.
description_plain: Your child can convert between units that measure the same thing within one measurement system.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems in real-world context involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions, using a variety of representations.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-world problems that involve dividing unit fractions by whole numbers and dividing whole numbers by unit fractions using different methods.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate what happens when a whole number is divided by a unit fraction.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients. Use the relationship between multiplication and division to justify conclusions.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate what happens when a unit fraction is divided by a whole number and explain the answer using multiplication and division relationships.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by fractions, and solve real-life problems using these skills.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems in real-world contexts involving multiplication of fractions, including mixed numbers, by using a variety of representations including equations and models.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-world problems by multiplying fractions, including mixed numbers, using pictures, equations, and other tools.

subject: Math
description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number; explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = n × a/n × b to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1.
description_plain: Your child can explain why multiplying by a fraction greater than 1 makes a number bigger and why multiplying by a fraction less than 1 makes it smaller, and understand how multiplying by 1 keeps a fraction the same.

subject: Math
description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can compare the size of a multiplication result to one of the numbers being multiplied by looking at the other number, without actually multiplying.

subject: Math
description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing), by:
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling a number.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of a rectangle with fractional sides by covering it with small squares and show that multiplying the side lengths gives the same area.

subject: Math
